_work.title: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
_work.creator: various
_work.date: Middle Kingdom – Roman period (c. 2000 BCE – 200 CE)
_work.rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The material culture of ancient Egyptian magic (heka): protective and funerary amulets — wedjat eyes, scarabs, djed pillars, tyet knots — alongside Book of the Dead vignettes, Horus cippi against venom, apotropaic ivory wands, and the figures (Bes, Taweret, Harpocrates) who guarded the living and the dead.

entries.title: Weighing of the Heart, Papyrus of Ani
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egyptian scribes and painters)
entries.summary:
The judgement scene from the *Papyrus of Ani*, the most celebrated copy of the Egyptian Book of the Dead, now in the British Museum.

## Iconography
Jackal-headed Anubis weighs the scribe Ani's heart against the feather of Maat on a great balance. Ibis-headed Thoth records the verdict, while the composite monster Ammit — part crocodile, lion and hippopotamus — waits to devour the heart of the unjust. Ani and his wife look on at the left.

## Significance
The *psychostasia* enacts spell 125 of the Book of the Dead, in which the deceased recites the "negative confession" before forty-two assessor gods. The scene is the visual heart of Egyptian funerary belief: the heart (*ib*), seat of conscience, must balance against cosmic order ([[motif:maat feather|Maat]]) for the dead to be reborn. Edited and translated by E. A. Wallis Budge (1895), the Ani papyrus shaped the modern Western image of Egyptian afterlife.

## For artists & game designers
The balance-scale judgement is a ready morality mechanic: weigh a character's deeds against a single feather, with a devourer waiting for failure.

citations.text: E. A. Wallis Budge, *The Book of the Dead: The Papyrus of Ani* (1895).

entries.title: Judgement Scene from the Papyrus of Hunefer
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egyptian scribes and painters)
entries.summary:
The weighing-of-the-heart vignette from the *Papyrus of Hunefer*, a royal scribe of Seti I, held in the British Museum.

## Iconography
Anubis leads Hunefer to the scales and adjusts the balance; the heart is checked against Maat's feather while Thoth notes the result and Ammit crouches beneath. At right, falcon-headed Horus presents the vindicated Hunefer to enthroned Osiris, flanked by Isis and Nephthys.

## Significance
The Hunefer scene is the textbook illustration of [[motif:funerary judgement|Egyptian judgement of the dead]], reproduced in nearly every survey of Egyptian art. It compresses spell 125 of the Book of the Dead into a continuous narrative: trial, verdict and presentation to Osiris, lord of the underworld. The crisp Ramesside draughtsmanship makes it the standard comparandum to the Ani papyrus.

## For artists & game designers
The left-to-right "trial then presentation to the throne" sequence is a clean storyboard for an afterlife judgement cutscene.

citations.text: John H. Taylor (ed.), *Journey through the Afterlife: Ancient Egyptian Book of the Dead* (2010).

entries.title: The Metternich Stela (Cippus of Horus)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt); commissioned by the priest Esatum
entries.date: c. 360–343 BCE (30th Dynasty, reign of Nectanebo II)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo Eb.hoop). Object public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The *Metternich Stela*, the largest and finest of the Horus-cippi, now in the Metropolitan Museum of Art (Fletcher Fund, 1950).

## Iconography
The young god Horus-the-child stands frontally on two crocodiles, grasping serpents, scorpions, a lion and an oryx in his fists, surmounted by the grotesque mask of Bes. The whole surface is densely covered with hieroglyphic spells against the bites of snakes, scorpions and other dangerous creatures.

## Significance
Cippi of Horus were healing devices: water poured over the carved spells absorbed their power and was drunk or applied to neutralise venom. The Metternich Stela preserves a long magical narrative in which Isis heals the poisoned infant Horus, the mythic precedent guaranteeing the cure. Studied by C. E. Sander-Hansen and central to Egyptian medical-magical practice, it shows [[motif:healing magic|magic and medicine]] as one continuous craft.

## For artists & game designers
A carved stone whose inscriptions "charge" poured water is a vivid, tactile healing-item mechanic.

citations.text: C. E. Sander-Hansen, *Die Texte der Metternichstele* (1956).

entries.title: Cippus with Pataikos on the Crocodiles (MET 30.8.87)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A magical stela (cippus) in the Metropolitan Museum on which the dwarf-god Pataikos replaces the usual Horus-child standing on the crocodiles, flanked by Isis and Nephthys.

## Iconography
A frontal apotropaic figure stands on two crocodiles, grasping serpents and noxious animals; here the body is the squat form of Pataikos rather than the youthful Horus. The protective goddesses Isis and Nephthys stand to either side, with magical inscriptions covering the field.

## Significance
The substitution of Pataikos shows how flexible the [[motif:horus on the crocodiles|Horus-on-the-crocodiles]] formula was: the protective power lay in the composition — mastery over dangerous creatures — more than in any single deity. Such cippi, poured over with water to heal snakebite, are catalogued among Egyptian healing magic and demonstrate the syncretic crowding of protective gods (Bes, Pataikos, Horus) into one apotropaic image.

## For artists & game designers
A single "slot" in a protective composition that different guardian figures can fill is a neat modular design pattern for ward objects.

citations.text: Heike Sternberg-el Hotabi, *Untersuchungen zur Überlieferungsgeschichte der Horusstelen* (1999).

entries.title: Child Horus Standing on Crocodiles (Healing Stela)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A photograph (Tupper / Boston Public Library) of a Horus-cippus showing the child god triumphant over crocodiles and venomous creatures.

## Iconography
The naked Horus-child, with the side-lock of youth, stands frontally upon two crocodiles. In his outstretched hands he grips serpents, scorpions and other dangerous animals, demonstrating total mastery over the agents of poison.

## Significance
The "Horus on the crocodiles" type (*Horus cippi* or *Horus stelae*) was the standard Egyptian device against snakebite and scorpion sting. Its logic is sympathetic: because the infant Horus survived every venomous threat under Isis's protection, water charged by the inscribed stela could transfer that immunity to a patient. Thousands survive, from monumental stelae to pocket amulets, making it one of the most widespread forms of practical [[motif:healing magic|Egyptian magic]].

## For artists & game designers
The child-who-grips-snakes-and-survives is a compact emblem of immunity — useful for an antidote item or a poison-resistant character sigil.

entries.title: Faience Amulet of the God Bes (Museo Egizio C 615)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A glazed faience amulet of the dwarf-god Bes in the Museo Egizio, Turin.

## Iconography
A squat, frontal dwarf deity with bandy legs, a lion-like bearded face, protruding tongue and a feathered crown. The deliberately grotesque face — meant to frighten away evil — is Bes's signature.

## Significance
Bes was a benevolent household protector, guarding sleepers, women in childbirth and young children against snakes, scorpions and malevolent spirits. Unlike most Egyptian gods he is shown full-face, confronting the viewer (and the threat) directly. His image proliferated on amulets, beds, headrests and mirror handles from the Middle Kingdom onward and survived into Greco-Roman [[motif:apotropaic magic|apotropaic magic]], underpinning the composite Bes-Pantheos figures.

## For artists & game designers
Bes is the archetype of the friendly-grotesque guardian: ugliness as a feature, protection through intimidation.

entries.title: Faience Wedjat-Eye Amulet (Museo Egizio C 1108)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A glazed faience *wedjat*-eye amulet — the Eye of Horus — in the Museo Egizio, Turin.

## Iconography
The stylised falcon's eye combines a human eyebrow and lid with the markings of a lanner falcon: the curling "teardrop" and the spiral below the eye. It is among the most instantly recognisable of all Egyptian symbols.

## Significance
The [[motif:wedjat|wedjat]] ("the whole / sound one") embodies the eye Horus lost in battle with Seth and had restored by Thoth — hence it signifies healing, wholeness and protection. Worn by the living and laid on mummies (often over the embalming incision), it was the commonest of all Egyptian amulets. Its six geometric parts were later read as fractions in scribal arithmetic, linking magic to measure.

## For artists & game designers
The wedjat is a near-universal "protection" glyph; its restored-from-injury backstory makes it a natural icon for a healing or shield power-up.

entries.title: Heart Scarab in Egyptian Blue (Museo Egizio C 6016)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A large heart scarab of Egyptian blue in the Museo Egizio, Turin, the type of funerary amulet placed over the mummy's heart.

## Iconography
A carved beetle (a dung-beetle, *Scarabaeus sacer*) with detailed wing-cases and legs; the flat underside of such scarabs was inscribed with a magical spell. Heart scarabs are notably larger than ordinary scarab seals.

## Significance
The heart scarab carried spell 30B of the Book of the Dead, addressing the heart: "do not stand as a witness against me" at the weighing of the heart. By magically silencing the heart's potential self-incrimination, the amulet insured the deceased against failing the judgement. The scarab form itself evoked Khepri, the self-renewing morning sun, making the object a double charm for [[motif:rebirth|rebirth]]. Carol Andrews's catalogue treats it as the funerary amulet par excellence.

## For artists & game designers
An object that literally bribes a character's own conscience to stay silent at judgement is a wonderfully ambiguous talisman.

entries.title: Heart Scarab of Bak-en-Djehuti (Walters 42380)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
The inscribed heart scarab of a man named Bak-en-Djehuti, in the Walters Art Museum, Baltimore.

## Iconography
A carved scarab beetle whose flat base bears a hieroglyphic inscription naming the owner and giving the heart-spell. The naming personalises the otherwise standardised amulet for a specific deceased individual.

## Significance
This scarab carries the version of Book of the Dead spell 30B that begins "O my heart which I had from my mother...," pleading with the heart not to betray its owner before the divine tribunal. Named heart scarabs let Egyptologists tie magical objects to particular biographies and to the spread of the spell across the New Kingdom. The Walters example is a clear teaching specimen of how text and amulet fused into a single funerary safeguard.

## For artists & game designers
Inscribing the owner's name turns a generic protective item into a bespoke relic — a small detail that makes loot feel personal.

citations.text: John H. Taylor (ed.), *Journey through the Afterlife* (2010).

entries.title: Pectoral with Winged Scarab (Museo Egizio C 6850)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A pectoral in the form of a winged scarab, in the Museo Egizio, Turin, of the type sewn onto the mummy's chest wrappings.

## Iconography
A central scarab beetle spreads broad outstretched wings to either side, often pushing or framing a solar disk. The winged scarab is a single composite emblem of the reborn sun.

## Significance
The scarab pushing the sun across the sky personified Khepri, "he who comes into being," the morning aspect of the sun-god and a guarantee of daily renewal. Placed as a winged pectoral over the heart, it transferred that promise of [[motif:rebirth|solar rebirth]] to the deceased. Winged scarab pectorals are among the most spectacular pieces of Egyptian funerary jewellery, fusing precious craft with potent resurrection symbolism.

## For artists & game designers
The winged-scarab-and-sun is a clean heraldic device for a faction of rebirth or dawn; it reads instantly even at small scale.

entries.title: Djed Pillar Amulet (Metropolitan Museum)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A *djed*-pillar amulet in the Metropolitan Museum of Art, one of the core funerary amulets prescribed by the Book of the Dead.

## Iconography
A columnar form crowned by four stacked horizontal bars or capitals. Variously interpreted as a stylised tree-trunk, a bundle of stalks, or — most enduringly — the backbone of Osiris.

## Significance
The [[motif:djed pillar|djed]] hieroglyph means "stability/endurance," and as the spine of Osiris it promised the deceased a stable, re-membered body and resurrection. Book of the Dead spell 155 calls for a gold djed at the throat of the mummy so "you raise up your backbone, O weary-hearted one." The "raising of the djed" was also a major royal festival affirming cosmic order. It is, with the wedjat and the tyet, one of the most frequently buried amulets.

## For artists & game designers
A spine-as-pillar glyph signifying "stability" is a strong icon for a fortification, anchor, or resurrection mechanic.

entries.title: Red Jasper Tyet Amulet (Knot of Isis, MET)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A red jasper *tyet* amulet — the "Knot of Isis" or "Blood of Isis" — in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## Iconography
A looped knot resembling the ankh but with its two side-arms folded downward, like a sash tied at the waist. The deep red of jasper or carnelian was the prescribed material.

## Significance
The [[motif:tyet|tyet]] is closely paired with the djed: where the djed gives Osirian stability, the tyet invokes the protective power of Isis. Book of the Dead spell 156 requires a tyet of red jasper at the mummy's throat so that "the power of Isis is the protection of this great one." Its red colour was read as the blood of Isis, a potent safeguarding fluid. The tyet, djed and wedjat together form the standard amuletic triad of Egyptian burial.

## For artists & game designers
Material-specified magic (it only works in *red* stone) is a clean crafting constraint: the colour is part of the spell.

entries.title: Faience Overseer Ushabti (Metropolitan Museum)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A glazed faience "overseer" *ushabti* in the Metropolitan Museum of Art — a magical servant figure for the afterlife.

## Iconography
A small mummiform (or, as an overseer, kilted and whip-bearing) figurine of bright faience, inscribed with a spell. Overseer ushabtis are shown in the dress of the living to command the gangs of worker ushabtis.

## Significance
Ushabtis ("answerers") were animated by spell 6 of the Book of the Dead: when the deceased was called to labour in the [[motif:field of reeds|Field of Reeds]], the figurine would answer "Here I am!" and do the work in their stead. Wealthy burials held hundreds — ideally 365 workers plus 36 overseers. They are the clearest case of Egyptian magic as labour-saving technology, substituting an enchanted proxy for the owner's own toil.

## For artists & game designers
A figurine that answers a summons and works on your behalf is a ready-made minion or familiar mechanic, complete with overseer hierarchy.

entries.title: Taweret Figure Vase with Human Head (MET)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A figure-vase in the form of the goddess Taweret (here with a human head) in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## Iconography
Taweret combines the body of a pregnant hippopotamus standing upright, the limbs and paws of a lion, the back and tail of a crocodile, and pendulous breasts and belly; this vessel substitutes a human head for the usual hippo head. She often rests a paw on the *sa* hieroglyph of protection.

## Significance
[[motif:taweret|Taweret]] ("the Great One") was, with Bes, the chief protector of women in pregnancy and childbirth and of infants. Her fearsome hybrid form channelled the danger of the hippopotamus into a guardian against the demons that threatened mother and child. Figure vases like this likely held milk or protective liquids used in birth ritual, fusing object and deity into a single magical implement.

## For artists & game designers
Taweret models the "dangerous animal turned protector" archetype: terrifying anatomy repurposed to guard the vulnerable.

entries.title: Apotropaic Wand ("Birth Tusk") Fragment (MET 55475)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A fragment of an Egyptian apotropaic wand, or "birth tusk," of carved hippopotamus ivory, in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## Iconography
A curved, flat blade of ivory (following the shape of a hippopotamus canine) incised with a procession of protective beings: Taweret, Bes, lions, snakes, knife-wielding genii and composite demons who ward off danger.

## Significance
These Middle Kingdom "magic knives" were used to draw a protective circle around a mother and newborn — traces of wear at the tips suggest they were dragged across the ground or a bed. The parade of fierce guardians, several gripping knives, formed a frontier no hostile spirit could cross. They are the principal evidence for [[motif:apotropaic magic|domestic protective magic]] around childbirth, studied in detail by Hartwig Altenmüller and Geraldine Pinch.

## For artists & game designers
A wand whose engraved monsters are the spell — a portable bestiary that doubles as a ward — is an evocative magic-item concept.

entries.title: Vignettes from the Greenfield Papyrus (Sheet 12)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egyptian scribes and painters)
entries.summary:
A strip of vignettes from the *Greenfield Papyrus*, the Book of the Dead of the priestess Nestanebetisheru, in the British Museum.

## Iconography
A register of small painted scenes (vignettes) illustrating the spells of the Book of the Dead — deities, the deceased in adoration, and ritual episodes — running above or beside columns of hieratic text.

## Significance
At over 37 metres, the Greenfield Papyrus is the longest known Book of the Dead. Made for a high-ranking woman of the Theban priestly elite around 950 BCE, it is a key witness to the 21st-Dynasty recension of funerary [[motif:book of the dead|spells]], when such papyri were produced for an aristocratic clientele. Its vignettes are an invaluable corpus of Egyptian afterlife iconography, named for Edith Mary Greenfield, who donated it in 1910.

## For artists & game designers
A scrolling band of captioned vignettes is a natural template for an in-world illuminated codex or a side-scrolling ritual sequence.

citations.text: E. A. Wallis Budge, *The Greenfield Papyrus in the British Museum* (1912).

entries.title: Scarab Seal of Imeni (Walters 42.21)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A steatite scarab seal naming a man called Imeni, in the Walters Art Museum, Baltimore.

## Iconography
A small beetle carved in the round on top, its flat underside engraved with a name and decorative design for use as a stamp seal. Pierced lengthwise, it could be strung as a ring or pendant.

## Significance
The scarab was simultaneously seal, amulet and sign: its dung-beetle form embodied Khepri, the self-generating sun, so even a humble administrative seal carried connotations of [[motif:rebirth|rebirth]] and good fortune. Middle Kingdom name- and title-scarabs like Imeni's document the merging of bureaucratic identity with protective magic. Scarab seals are among the most abundant Egyptian artifacts and a backbone of dating and prosopography for Egyptologists.

## For artists & game designers
An object that is at once an ID badge, a lucky charm and a signature stamp compresses identity, magic and authority into one tiny prop.

entries.title: Scarab Engravings, *The Sacred Beetle* (1902)
entries.work: Ancient Egyptian Magic, Amulets & Funerary Protection
entries.creator: John Ward (author/illustrator of the treatise)
entries.rights: Public domain (No restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons / Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
An illustration from John Ward's *The Sacred Beetle: A Popular Treatise on Egyptian Scarabs in Art and History* (1902), documenting types of scarab.

## Iconography
A printed antiquarian plate showing rows of scarabs in profile and underside view, recording the variety of forms, inscriptions and designs of these beetle amulets.

## Significance
Ward's treatise was part of the turn-of-the-century antiquarian enthusiasm that turned the scarab into the emblem of ancient Egypt for Western collectors. Such typological plates both popularised the [[motif:scarab|scarab]] and fed the Egyptian-revival and reception interest that later flowed into occult and Theosophical circles. The image is thus a useful witness to how Egyptian magical objects were catalogued, reproduced and re-enchanted in the modern era.

## For artists & game designers
A period engraved "catalogue plate" of magical objects is a handy in-world artefact: a collector's reference sheet the player can consult.
